feedback.text: The issues underlined in the Digital Fairness Act would already be great for online consumer protection, but there is still a gap that isn't yet covered. Right now companies that sell digital media, including video games but also e-books, music, etc. are able to revoke a consumer's access at any time even if said digital media was bought, not rented. An example of this is Amazon being able to revoke access to sold e-books, the equivalent of an author destroying copies of their books if they didn't want the owners of those books to read them anymore. This is obviously terrible for media preservation and betrays consumers in a way that would be illegal if the product was physical, but the issue becomes even worse when applied to video games: Revoking access to a video game is the most effective way to force consumers to buy new games worse than what they already own, and games that include the predatory practices that the Digital Fairness Act is aiming to deal with. Revoking access to bought digital media is the digital equivalent to the worst forms of planned obsolescence, and is arguably just as bad as the issues that the Digital Fairness Act is already aiming to deal with. The "Stop Destroying Games" EU Citizen's Initiative aims to tackle this issue with regards to specifically video games, but this initiative could be another way to return the right of ownership of digital media to consumers, or at least prevent such destructive practices. I have had some personal experience with this issue when support ended for the video game Robocraft 2. Support of this game ended due to Freejam, the developers, giving up on the project. Some members of the community were able to revive the game using community servers, but the growth of the community has been stifled by the fact that at any time those community members could be given a legally binding order from what remains of Freejam (which has since been liquidated) to end their support and destroy the work they've done to revive the game forever. Some legal security for this and similar games with community support would only be fair to those who play them.
